narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, an LSAT score of 35, middle-family income background, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, both at the lowest 10th percentile. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of similar students, estimated an 84.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam—far higher than the typical low-risk profile that leads to acceptance. This high predicted failure risk directly led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass the bar on their first try. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers—much lower than the average student's 5th or 6th decile—and this low performance strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom 10% versus the typical mid-range ranking, reinforcing the model's concern about early academic struggles carrying over. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, slightly above the average of 3.21, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction a bit toward passing success, showing some strength there. While your LSAT was close to the average of 36.7 and your full-time status aligns with most students, these didn't outweigh the dominant impact of those low decile rankings. Compared to typical accepted students, who often have mid-to-high decile placements signaling consistent performance, your profile raised red flags about long-term bar readiness. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic leader in law—many from similar backgrounds succeed with targeted improvements—but the model saw your grades trajectory as the biggest hurdle. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or retakes, consider LSAT prep for a higher score, and seek bar prep resources early; reapplying after demonstrating upward trends could shift your prediction dramatically. You're capable of turning this around—many do.
